# Table of contents

# Methodology

- Each file is a full `VirtualMachineConfigOption` export for one hardware version (`<version>vmx-N</version>`), with one `<guestOSDescriptor>` per supported guest ID.

- Comparisons use all XML fields under each descriptor **except** `<cpuFeatureMask>` blocks (mask strings change often and obscure higher-level capability questions).

- **Detailed timelines** (below) list **every** guest ID and **every** flattened descriptor field (again excluding CPU masks) across vmx-10–vmx-22.

- **“New at vmx-N”** means the guest ID is present in the vmx-N file but was absent from the vmx-(N−1) file (including reintroductions after a gap).

# Snapshot metadata

| vmx | ESXi / VM line (from `<description>`) | Guest count |
|-----|----------------------------------------|-------------|
| vmx-10 | ESXi 5.5 virtual machine | 104 |
| vmx-11 | ESXi 6.0 virtual machine | 112 |
| vmx-12 | Workstation 12 virtual machine | 113 |
| vmx-13 | ESXi 6.5 virtual machine | 126 |
| vmx-14 | ESXi 6.7 virtual machine | 141 |
| vmx-15 | ESXi 6.7 U2 virtual machine | 140 |
| vmx-16 | Workstation 15 virtual machine | 141 |
| vmx-17 | ESXi 7.0 virtual machine | 145 |
| vmx-18 | ESXi 7.0 U1 virtual machine | 157 |
| vmx-19 | ESXi 7.0 U2 virtual machine | 158 |
| vmx-20 | ESXi 8.0 virtual machine | 172 |
| vmx-21 | ESXi 8.0 U2 virtual machine | 173 |
| vmx-22 | ESXi 9.0 virtual machine | 187 |

- **Distinct guest IDs** appearing in any file: **189**.

# Guest IDs introduced (or reintroduced) at each hardware version

vmx-10 is the baseline snapshot in this corpus: every guest listed for vmx-10 is “new” relative to an empty prior set.

## Reintroductions after removal

These IDs were absent in at least one intermediate hardware version, then appeared again in a later file (listed as “new” in the section above):

- `crxPod1Guest`: first in vmx-14, removed vmx-15–vmx-18, back from vmx-19 through vmx-22.

# Guest IDs removed between consecutive hardware versions

- **`rhel7Guest`**: present in vmx-10, absent in vmx-11 (the 64-bit `rhel7_64Guest` remains from vmx-11 onward).

- **`sles12Guest`**: present in vmx-10, absent in vmx-11 (`sles12_64Guest` remains).

- **`crxPod1Guest`**: present in vmx-14, absent in vmx-15 (see reintroduction at vmx-19 above).

# Do existing guest IDs gain capabilities at newer hardware versions?

**Yes.** For guests that exist in both vmx-10 and vmx-22, **97 of 102** show at least one change among a focused set of numeric/boolean fields (`supportedMaxCPUs`, memory limits, hot-plug, secure boot, TPM, persistent memory flags, `supportedForCreate`, etc.). Across **all** non-mask fields, **663** (guest × consecutive-version) pairs differ.

## `supportedNumDisks`

In this dataset, **`supportedNumDisks` is 16 for every guest in every hardware version**—there are **no** step-ups (e.g. 4 → 5 disks) between vmx-10 and vmx-22.

## CPU, socket, core, and memory limits

- **`supportedMaxCPUs`**, **`supportedMaxMemMB`**, **`numSupportedPhysicalSockets`**, and **`numSupportedCoresPerSocket`** frequently increase at specific vmx boundaries (not necessarily every version).

- Example: `windows9Server64Guest` goes from **64 → 128** max CPUs at vmx-11, **128 → 256** at vmx-15, and **256 → 960** at vmx-22; `supportedMaxMemMB` steps **1,035,264 → 4,177,920** (vmx-11), **6,275,072** (vmx-13), **25,149,440** (vmx-17).

- Among guests present in both vmx-10 and vmx-22, **72** have at least one step where `supportedMaxCPUs` increases.

## Feature flags and nested options

Booleans and nested “supported/default” pairs often flip when the platform adds support—for example **`supportsHotPlugPCI`**, **`supportsSecureBoot`**, **`supportsTPM20`**, **`vvtdSupported/supported`**, **`vsgxSupported/supported`**, **`persistentMemorySupported`**, and **`supportedForCreate`**.

## Enumerated hardware (controllers, NICs, firmware, USB)

Multi-valued lists (`supportedDiskControllerList`, `supportedEthernetCard`, `supportedUSBControllerList`, `supportedFirmware`) gain or lose entries between versions. **20** guests present in both vmx-10 and vmx-22 see a change in the **count** of `supportedEthernetCard` values at some step (not only vmx-22); some counts go **up**, some **down** when VMware adjusts the catalog.

## New XML fields on older guests

Later hardware exports can add elements that were not emitted for the same logical guest at vmx-10—for example `ubuntu64Guest` gains **`wakeOnLanEthernetCard`** and persistent-memory sizing fields (`supportedMinPersistentMemoryMB`, `supportedMaxPersistentMemoryMB`, `recommendedPersistentMemoryMB`, `persistentMemoryColdGrowthGranularityMB`) by vmx-22.

# Detailed timelines (all guest IDs)

Each subsection is one guest ID. **Rows** are flattened XML field names (nested elements use `/` paths). **Columns** are hardware versions. `—` means the guest ID is not present in that snapshot or the field is absent. `<cpuFeatureMask>` is omitted. Multi-valued elements are shown as comma-separated lists (sorted). Pipe characters in raw values are replaced with `∣` so Markdown tables stay valid.

**First-appearance highlighting (cumulative):** For each guest and field, a value is highlighted the **first time it appears anywhere in that guest’s row** (scanning vmx-10 → vmx-22, including snapshots where the guest is absent in between—history still counts after a gap). For multi-valued fields (e.g. `supportedFirmware`), only **new** tokens are wrapped in `<span class="new-feature-highlight">…</span>`; tokens already seen in an earlier column stay plain. For scalars, the whole cell is marked the first time that exact value appears. **vmx-10** is never highlighted so the baseline column stays readable. Reappearing values later in the timeline are not highlighted again.

# Limitations

- **CPU feature masks** are excluded; they can change even when summarized limits stay the same.

- Files are **static fixtures**, not live vCenter responses; absolute numbers match whatever was captured when the XML was generated.

- **Guest count** can drop at a version when an ID is removed, then rise again when IDs are added or reintroduced (e.g. vmx-15 vs vmx-14).

- The **Detailed timelines** section is large (~13k lines); some Markdown viewers may be slow. Search for a guest ID (each guest is a level-2 heading) to jump quickly.
